Quiz 1 • Quiz 1 at the beginning of Lab 3 • Bring: ⎻ A ballpen and correction tape or a pencil and eraser ⎻ Any printed materials (Open book, no electronic device e.g., laptop) ⎻ Student card (Tutor will verify) • Each lab will use different questions, but at the same difficulty level • Covers content of Weeks 1 and 2 • No discussion; no collaboration • 7 multiple choice questions; 12 minutes; 7.5% of final grade • Only 1 answer for each question; no negative mark for wrong answers  Finding Course Information Moodle EdStem • Pre-class activities • Course Announcements • Lecture slides • Live class activities (lecture • Lecture recording (after) and lab) • Jupyter Notebook • Assessments • Discussion (all content- • Final Exam related questions) Monty Python’s Flying Circus Python IDEs • Jupyter Notebook — the one we are using ⎻ Built-in to Ed — suitable for “sandbox” use ⎻ Available in Anaconda Navigator — for ⎻ Built-in to Google Colab • PyCharm • Spyder Jupyter Notebook (in Ed)

Results Code

Text Jupyter Notebook

Text

Code

Results

Operators • Operators computations like addition, division, is-equal-to, less-than, and

• Operands values that an operator is applied to Example operator

20 + 32

operands Expression • A combination of values and (often) operators that the interpreter can evaluate to produce a (new) value

365 / 7 5 < 9 (3 < 5) and (3 > 5) Variables • A named place to store results of an expression classSize = 360 numGroups = 360 / 5 Variable Names • May include letters, numerals, and underscore (_) • May not begin with a numeral • By convention ⎻ begin with lower-case letter ⎻ Use either upper-case or _ to mark off words (when used) • Be consistent More conventional Less conventional labClassSize = 360 labclasssize = 360 lab_class_size = 360 LabClassSize = 360 Variables in Expressions • Expressions may include variables numGroups = classSize / 5 area = height * width area = radius * pi**2

Arithmetic Operators Operator Operation Example Result + Addition 11 + 3 14

- Subtraction 11 – 3 8

* Multiplication 11 * 3 33

/ Division (float) 11.0 / 3.0 3.666666666667

// Floor/Integer Division 11//3 3 -11//3 -4 % Remainder (mod) 11 % 3 2

** Exponentiation 11**3 1331

Selecting the Correct Operator Task: write code to divide 51 pieces of candy equally among 6 kids.

• How many pieces does each kid get? ⎻ Result should be integer (why?) ⎻ Correct operator: //

• Are any pieces left over? ⎻ Result should be boolean (why?) ⎻ Correct operator: % + convert to type bool

• How many pieces are remaining? ⎻ Result should be integer (why?) ⎻ Correct operator: %

Result of / is always type float The result of division (/) is always float, whether operands are integer, float, or a combination of both

print(6/2) print(5/2) print(6.0/2.0) print(5.0/2.0) print(2/6.0) print(2/6)

Operation Precedence Rules • From high to low) Highest 1. Subexpressions within parentheses ( … ) 2. Exponentiation — powers & square roots 3. Multiplication, division, & remainder — performed from left to right 4. Addition & subtraction — performed from left to right Lowest

3 + (7 + 2) / 3**2 - 10 Operation Precedence Rules 3 + (7 + 2) / 3**2 – 10

3 + 9 / 3**2 – 10

3 + 9 / 9 – 10

3 + 1 - 10

4 - 10

-6

Arithmetic Operators?

Relational Operators 29

Relational operators compare the values of two expressions Applies to both int and float Is equal to Is not equal Is greater Is less then Is greater Is less than to than than or equal or equal to to a == b a != b a > b a < b a >= b a <= b  Relational Operators Syntax Semantics Example Result 30 == Equal to 5 == 6 False 5 == 5 True != Not equal to 5 != 6 True 5 != 5 False > Greater than 5>6 False 6>5 True < Less than 6<5 False 5<6 True >= Greater than or equal to 5 >= 5 True 5 >= 4 True 5 >= 6 False <= Less than or equal to 5 <= 5 True 5 <= 6 True 5 <= 4 False Exercise Try the code below:

print(2 == 1) print(1 > 1) print(1 >= 1) print(2 != 1) print("a" != 1) Print(False == 0)

Relational Operators?

Logical Operators • Logical operators include and, or, not

• Sometimes we want to check more than one condition at once, e.g., we might want to check if condition1 and condition2 are both True

• The semantics (meaning) of these operators is similar to their meaning in English, e.g., x > 0 and x < 10 is true only if x is greater than 0 and less than 10

• Precedence rules: not > and > or How to Evaluate Logical Operators x y x and y • The and statement is only True False False False when both conditions are true False True False and True False False True True True

x y x or y • The or statement is True if one or False False False both conditions are True. • The or statement is False if both False True True or conditions are false True False True True True True

• The not statement reverses the x not x truth value not False True True False Exercise Try below the statements:

a = True b = True c = False

print(a and b) print(a or b) print(not a) print(not b)

print(a or b and c) # Do we go from left to right? # What's the best way to avoid confusion? Logical Operators?

Strings • A string is a sequence of characters, e.g., 'banana’ • In Python, strings in python are surrounded by single or double quotation marks 'hello' or "hello" • A string can include spaces or digits 'hello1' 'hello hello' • A string can include special characters 'hello, world' 'hello !!!' • You can display a string literal with the print() function print("Hello") print('Hello') Single vs. Double Quotes • These are equivalent: quotes_single = 'a string' quotes_double = "a string”

• These are not permitted: quotes_mixed = 'a string” quotes_mixed = "a string’ Quotes within Strings • Why do these generate an error message? myString1 = "We are the so-called "Vikings" from the north." myString2 = "I am 5'4""

• To insert characters that are “illegal” in a string, use the escape character (\) Escape Characters and Sequence • The backslash character (\) is used to “escape” a special character

• Place the backslash character in front of the character we want to “escape” print("I am 5’8\"") #I am 5’8” print ("We are the so-called \"Vikings\" from the north.") #We are the so-called "Vikings" from the north.

• Backslash character plus character we want to “escape” is called an escape sequence

• Escape sequence is used to represent characters that are difficult or impossible to type directly in the code or that have a special meaning in Python Common Escape Sequences

Escape Sequence Purpose \' Print a single quote \" Print a double quote \\ Print a backslash \t Print a tab \n Print a newline (the “enter” key) Common Escape Sequences (Cont.) str1 = "\tI'm tabbed in." print(str1) I'm tabbed in. \t adds a tab

str2 = "We are splitting\na line." print(str2) \n adds a newline We are splitting a line.

str3 = "I'm \\ a \\ good boy." print(str3) \\ adds a single backslash I'm \ a \ good boy. Three Ways to Print Out Quotes in Str

print("I am 5’8\"") escape double quotes (using " for the entire string)

print('I am 5\’8"') escape single quotes (using ' for the entire string)

print("I am 5\’8\"") escape both single and double quotes (works for both ' and ") Alternative Solution: Triple Quotes Triple quotes can enclose strings containing single and double quotes

print('''She said, "Thank you! It's mine."''') #She said, "Thank you! It's mine."

print('''I am 5’8"''') #I am 5’8" Triple Quotes: Multi-line String • Triple quotes also used to represent multi-line strings: print('''Hello World !'’’)

• Equivalent using escape sequences print('Hello\nWorld\n!') Triple Quotes Example todo_list= """ When using triple quotes, the times I'll do a list of things: you hit “enter” inside the string will \t* read print as newlines \t* write \t* report \t puts in a tab """ print(todo_list)

Triple quotes are not an escape sequence but are helpful dealing with quotes in a string Strings?

Types of String Operations • Concatenation using + : str1 = ”Steve Jobs" str2 = " is the best" statement1 = str1 + str2 print(statement1) #Steve Jobs is the best

• Repetition using * : "abc" * 3 # arithmetic operator * on string my_name = ”Steve Jobs " new_name = 3 * my_name print(new_name) Steve Jobs Steve Jobs Steve Jobs Mixing Numeric and String Operands

x = 2 y = "hello" print(x + y) # What happens?

x = 2 y = "hello" print(x * y) # What happens? String Indexing • Access individual characters in a string with square bracket operator (number in brackets called index):

letter = 'fruit’[1] # = 'r'

• Index of first character is 0 String Indexing Name = ”Elon Musk"

String E l o n M u s k Index 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 Name[6] What is the printout? String Indexing from End Name = ”Elon Musk"

String E l o n M u s k Index -9 -8 -7 -6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1 Name[-3] What is the printout? String Indexing for Escape Sequence • Escape sequences look like two characters to us • But Python treats them as a single character 0 1 2 3 example1 = "see\n" s e e \n 0 1 2 3 example2 = "\tsee" \t s e e String Slicing • A segment of a string is called a slice. Selecting a slice is similar to selecting a character, but we are selecting multiple characters s = 'Monty Python' print(s[0:5]) print(s[6:12])

• The operator returns the part of the string from the "n-th" character to the "m-th" character, including the first but excluding the last An Example of String Slicing Name = ”Elon Musk"

String E l o n M u s k

Index 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

firstName = Name[0:5] # how to do this in Excel? lastName = Name[6:9] # how to do this in Excel? String Methods (Functions that belong to the string type) upper() lower() "Elon Musk" String A count() index() split() ”Elon Musk".lower() Method lstrip() rstrip() strip()

String B startswith() 'elon musk' endswith()  Common String Methods 61

string = ' Steve Jobs '

Method Description Result string.upper() Return copy of string in upper case ' STEVE JOBS ' string.lower() Return copy of string in lower case ' steve jobs ' string.strip() Returns a copy of the string with leading and 'Steve Jobs' trailing spaced stripped string.lstrip() Return a left-trimmed copy of the string ** 'Steve Jobs '

string.rstrip() Return a right-trimmed copy of the string ** ' Steve Jobs'

** may specify a character to trim (.lstrip("*")); space is default  Common String Methods (Cont.) 62

string = ' Steve Jobs '

Method Description Result string.count(…) Return number of occurrences of > string.count(" ") specified value in the string string.index(…) Search the string for a specified value > string.index("Jo") and return the position of where it was found string.startswith() Returns true if the string starts with the > string.startswith("S") False specified value string.endswith() Returns true if the string ends with the > string.endswith(” ") True specified value string.split() Splits the string using a specified ' Steve Jobs' separator (by default a whitespace), and returns a list – another data type String Length len() is a built-in function that returns the number of characters in a string

len(”Elon Musk") # returns 9 s = "Hello\nWorld" # \n is newline character len(s) # Returns 11

\n is two symbols, but it represents one character Is it a string method, i.e., does len() belong to string type? greet1 = "Hello World" greet2 = "How are you?" print(greet1.upper()) print(greet1.lower()) print(len(greet1)) print(greet1.index ("o")) print(greet1.count('l')) print(greet1[2:7]) print(greet1.startswith ("Hello")) print(greet2.endswith ("you?")) greetwords = greet1.split(" ") print('greetwords[0] = ', greetwords[0]) print('greetwords[1] = ', greetwords[1]) What is the printout? Recap Exercise greet1 = " Hello World " new_greet = greet1.replace("World", ”Eric") print(new_greet) print(new_greet.lstrip()) print(new_greet.rstrip()) print(new_greet.strip())

What is the printout? Recap Exercise Assume the following assignment statements: width = 20 height = 12.0

Write the value and data type of each expression) width//2 width/2.0 height/3 1 + 2 ** 5

Use the Python interpreter to check your answers Recap Exercise • Addition (+) for String means concatenation print('a' + 'b') # what is the printout?

• Multiplication (*) for String means multiple concatenation print('a' * 4) # what is the printout? String Operations? For this week’s lab
